questions.question: Explain the critical section problem and state the three fundamental requirements that a solution must satisfy.
keyPoints: Define critical section as the portion of code accessing shared resources
Mutual exclusion: only one process in CS at a time
Progress: processes not in RS can participate in decision making
Bounded waiting: limit on number of times other processes can enter after request

questions.question: What is a race condition? Illustrate with a simple example involving two processes modifying a shared variable.
keyPoints: Race condition: outcome depends on timing of concurrent processes
Example: two processes incrementing a shared counter
Without synchronization: read-modify-write can interleave causing lost updates
Show timeline showing interleaving and incorrect final value

questions.question: Differentiate between a mutex and a binary semaphore. Under what circumstances would you prefer one over the other?
keyPoints: Mutex provides ownership - only locking process can unlock
Binary semaphore is a signaling mechanism without ownership
Prefer mutex when: resource must be released by same process, recursive locking needed
Prefer semaphore when: any process can signal, or counting needed

questions.question: Explain the working of the Test-and-Set instruction for implementing mutual exclusion. Why is atomicity crucial for this instruction?
keyPoints: Test-and-set atomically tests and sets a boolean in one operation
Returns old value while setting lock to true
Atomicity prevents race condition between test and set
Without atomicity: multiple processes could see lock as false and enter CS

questions.question: What is busy waiting (spinlock)? Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. When is it appropriate to use spinlocks?
keyPoints: Busy waiting: process loops checking lock instead of blocking
Advantages: no context switch overhead, good for multiprocessors
Disadvantages: wastes CPU cycles
Best for very short critical sections in multi-processor systems

questions.question: Explain the producer-consumer synchronization problem. How can semaphores be used to solve it?
keyPoints: Producer creates items, consumer removes items
Shared buffer of finite capacity
Need to track: empty slots (counting semaphore), full slots (counting semaphore)
Mutex for mutual exclusion on buffer access

questions.question: What is a monitor in process synchronization? How does it provide mutual exclusion automatically?
keyPoints: Monitor is a high-level synchronization construct
Combines shared data and procedures into one module
Only one process active in monitor at any time
Uses condition variables for waiting and signaling
Automatic mutual exclusion at method entry
(a) Peterson's algorithm for two processes
(b) flag[i]=true indicates desire to enter, turn decides who goes when both want in
(c) Works only for 2 processes, assumes atomic memory operations, not suitable for modern architectures with weak memory models

questions.question: Three philosophers sit around a round table with a fork between each pair. Each philosopher thinks and then eats. However, a philosopher needs both adjacent forks to eat. This is the Dining Philosophers problem. (a) Explain why a naive solution leads to deadlock. (b) Design a solution using semaphores that prevents deadlock. (c) Analyze your solution for starvation.
keyPoints: (a) Deadlock: each picks left fork simultaneously, all wait forever for right fork
(b) Solution options: waiter/arbitrator (mutex), odd-even strategy, or resource hierarchy
(c) With proper design, can guarantee no starvation or bounded waiting
Show semaphore initialization and algorithm

questions.question: (a) Define atomicity and explain why it is critical in synchronization primitives. (b) How does hardware help in achieving atomicity? (c) Compare test-and-set and compare-and-swap instructions for implementing locks.
keyPoints: (a) Atomicity: operation executes as single indivisible unit
(b) Hardware provides atomic instructions, memory barriers, bus locking
(c) TAS: returns old value while setting, simple but causes contention
CAS: compares expected with actual, swaps only if match, more flexible
Compare implementation and use cases
